question: A CI/CD pipeline step runs Claude Code to review PRs. The step hangs for 30+ minutes on every PR. What is wrong?
options: Claude Code needs more tokens to complete the review
The -p / --print flag is missing — Claude Code is in interactive mode waiting for keyboard input
The review criteria are too complex for automated processing
The GitHub API rate limit is being hit
explanation: Without the -p (--print) flag, Claude Code runs in interactive mode and waits for user input. In a CI/CD pipeline there is no keyboard — the step hangs indefinitely until killed by a timeout. The -p flag makes Claude Code process the prompt, output the result, and exit. This is the most common CI/CD integration mistake.
whyWrong: Token limits cause API errors with specific error messages — not an indefinite hang
This is the correct answer
Review complexity doesn't cause hanging — Claude would either complete or time out on complexity
GitHub API rate limits return specific HTTP 429 errors — not a pipeline hang

question: Code was generated by Claude in Session A. A review request is sent to Claude in the same Session A. Reviews consistently miss bugs introduced during generation. Why?
options: The review prompt needs more specific bug-finding criteria
Self-review bias — Session A retains the reasoning context from generation, making it unlikely to question its own decisions
The context window is too full after generation for quality review
Session A doesn't have access to the test suite for validation
explanation: In the same session, Claude retains the mental model of WHY each decision was made during generation. This makes it far less likely to question those decisions during review — even if the decisions were wrong. Independent review requires a fresh Claude instance (separate API call) with no knowledge of the generation process.
whyWrong: Better criteria help but don't address the fundamental bias from shared reasoning context
This is the correct answer — the same-session bias is the root cause
Context window fill can degrade quality but doesn't explain consistently missing the SAME bugs that were introduced
Test suite access is useful but not the root cause — the session bias is

question: A PR has 15 changed files. Which review approach produces better security findings?
options: Send all 15 files in one Claude call for comprehensive analysis
Per-file reviews (parallel) followed by a cross-file integration review (sequential)
Run 3 separate reviews of all 15 files and take the union of findings
Focus the review on files with the most line changes
explanation: Multi-pass review: per-file reviews run in parallel with full context window attention per file, then cross-file synthesis identifies integration issues. All-at-once review suffers from attention dilution — each file gets 1/15th of effective attention. Running 3 reviews of all files doesn't fix the dilution problem.
whyWrong: All-at-once causes attention dilution — each file gets fractional attention
This is the correct answer
Running the same diluted review 3 times doesn't fix the attention distribution problem
Files with most changes may not contain the most critical security issues

question: A code review pipeline should output findings that can be parsed programmatically to make merge/block decisions. Which output format and command-line option combination is correct?
options: Plain text output, parse with grep
--output-format json, parse findings with jq or Python JSON parser
Markdown output, extract with regex
YAML output, --output-format yaml
explanation: --output-format json returns structured JSON output that can be reliably parsed by jq, Python, or any JSON parser. Plain text and markdown require regex parsing which is fragile and breaks when output format varies slightly. Programmatic gating (block merge if CRITICAL findings exist) requires machine-parseable structured output.
whyWrong: grep on plain text is fragile and breaks with any variation in Claude's output phrasing
This is the correct answer
Regex on markdown is fragile — structured JSON is designed for programmatic parsing
YAML output is not a supported --output-format option for Claude Code

question: A code review integration is flagging 50 findings per PR, mostly related to code style. Developers have disabled it. What should change?
options: Increase review frequency to normalize the high finding count
Implement specific, verifiable criteria with explicit scope exclusions (e.g., 'do not flag formatting — handled by linter')
Reduce the finding limit to 10 per PR, randomly selecting the most important
Switch to a different Claude model with better code judgment
explanation: High false positive rates kill developer trust and adoption. The fix: explicit, verifiable criteria ('functions > 30 lines = Medium finding') with explicit exclusions ('do NOT flag: naming style, comment formatting, import ordering — we have automated linters for these'). This reduces noise dramatically while keeping real signal.
whyWrong: More frequency makes the problem worse — developers are already ignoring it
This is the correct answer
Randomly selecting 10 doesn't reduce false positives — it just reduces the count while keeping the same bad ratio
Model capability isn't the issue — criteria specificity is

question: A review pipeline has three stages: security, coverage, and architecture. Each stage should see only relevant context. Which design is correct?
options: All three stages receive identical context for consistency
Security stage receives security rules + changed files; coverage stage receives coverage data + changed files; architecture stage receives CLAUDE.md + changed files + prior security findings
Each stage receives the full output from all previous stages
All three stages share a single context that accumulates across stages
explanation: Specialized stages receive only relevant context. Security: security rules + changed files. Coverage: coverage report + changed files. Architecture: CLAUDE.md conventions + changed files + prior security findings (for awareness). Identical context dilutes focus. Full accumulation across stages re-introduces noise from earlier stages.
whyWrong: Identical context means every stage is distracted by irrelevant rules from other domains
This is the correct answer
Full previous stage output re-introduces noise — each stage should receive a curated subset
Accumulating context across stages means the final stage processes a large noisy context — attention dilution

question: A code review finds a potential security issue but has low confidence — the pattern looks like SQL injection but could be a parameterized query with unusual syntax. How should this finding be handled?
options: Report it as CRITICAL — better to flag false positives than miss real vulnerabilities
Suppress it — low-confidence findings shouldn't appear in automated reviews
Report with severity + confidence level, route to human security expert for verification before blocking merge
Ask Claude to retry with a more focused prompt to increase confidence
explanation: Low-confidence findings on potentially critical issues should be reported with their confidence level and routed to human verification — not suppressed (misses real issues) and not automatically elevated to CRITICAL (causes false positive fatigue). The human security expert has context to distinguish the pattern.
whyWrong: Escalating all low-confidence findings to CRITICAL creates severe false positive fatigue
Suppressing low-confidence findings on potential security issues risks missing real vulnerabilities
This is the correct answer
Retry may not improve confidence if the ambiguity is inherent in the code pattern — human judgment is needed

question: A team wants a reusable comprehensive security audit that reads many files, runs grep across the codebase, and produces a detailed report. Should this be a Slash Command or Skill?
options: Slash Command — it's triggered with a simple /security-audit invocation
Skill with context: fork — verbose exploration runs isolated, main session receives only the structured report
Either works — both can perform the same analysis
Neither — security audits should use the Claude API directly, not Claude Code features
explanation: A comprehensive security audit reads many files and produces verbose intermediate output. Running this in the main session (Slash Command) would fill the context with file contents and grep results. A Skill with context: fork runs in an isolated sub-session — all the verbose work stays isolated, and the main session receives only the structured security report.
whyWrong: Slash Command runs in the main session — the verbose file reading fills the main context window
This is the correct answer
They are NOT equivalent — context isolation is the key difference for verbose discovery tasks
Claude Code Skills are purpose-built for exactly this use case — isolated verbose analysis

question: A severity system has most findings classified as CRITICAL. Developers treat all findings as medium-priority since CRITICAL is too common. What does this indicate?
options: The codebase has genuinely critical security issues requiring immediate attention
Severity inflation — criteria for CRITICAL are too broad, causing the severity to lose meaning
Developers need training to take CRITICAL findings seriously
The review scope is too narrow, causing valid critical findings to cluster
explanation: Severity inflation: when too many findings are CRITICAL, the severity loses meaning and developers treat all of them as medium. Healthy distribution: ~5% CRITICAL, ~20% HIGH, ~50% MEDIUM, ~25% LOW. When CRITICAL is common, the criteria need narrowing. CRITICAL should mean 'ship blocker — fix immediately.'
whyWrong: If the codebase genuinely had mostly critical issues, the team would likely know from incidents
This is the correct answer
Developer behavior is a signal about the system design, not a training problem
Narrow scope causing clustering would produce findings concentrated in specific areas, not globally inflated severity

question: A team wants code review rules that apply only to TypeScript files in the API layer but not to TypeScript test files. Which mechanism is most precise?
options: A directory CLAUDE.md in src/api/ that covers all TypeScript files
A path-specific rule with paths: ['src/api/**/*.ts', '!src/api/**/*.test.ts', '!src/api/**/*.spec.ts']
A project-level CLAUDE.md section with conditional logic for file types
Two Slash Commands — one for API files and one for test files
explanation: Path-specific rules with glob patterns and exclusion patterns (!) provide the most precise targeting. The directory CLAUDE.md would apply to ALL TypeScript files including tests. Project-level CLAUDE.md applies globally. Slash Commands are user-invoked, not automatic. The glob pattern with exclusions is exactly designed for this precision.
whyWrong: Directory CLAUDE.md applies to all files in the directory — can't exclude test files specifically
This is the correct answer
Project-level CLAUDE.md doesn't support file-type conditionals — it applies globally
Slash Commands are manually invoked — they don't provide automatic file-type targeting in CI/CD

question: A code review finds potential interface contract mismatches between two files. The per-file reviewer flagged it in one file but not the other. How should the review pipeline handle this?
options: The per-file review of the second file should be re-run with knowledge of the first file's finding
The cross-file integration review identifies contradictions between per-file findings and surfaces them for human resolution
The finding from the first file is sufficient — the second file's reviewer can be ignored
Both per-file reviews should be re-run together in a single combined context
explanation: Cross-file review is specifically for identifying inter-file issues that per-file reviewers can't see in isolation. The cross-file pass receives structured summaries from all per-file reviews, detects contradictions (File A assumes X, File B assumes Y), and surfaces them for human resolution. This is the canonical purpose of the two-pass review architecture.
whyWrong: Re-running with cross-file knowledge would break per-file isolation — the cross-file pass handles integration
This is the correct answer
Ignoring the second file's reviewer creates asymmetric handling — the contradiction needs surfacing
Running both in one context re-introduces attention dilution — use the cross-file pass instead

question: A headless Claude Code review step has access to all 20 available tools including file_write and file_delete. What is wrong with this configuration?
options: 20 tools is within the reliable selection range — no issue
In non-interactive mode, there's no ability to intervene if Claude uses file_write or file_delete inappropriately — restrict to minimum necessary tools
The file_write tool is needed to write the review report
Headless mode automatically restricts dangerous tools — no action needed
explanation: In non-interactive mode, there is no human to intervene if Claude calls a dangerous tool unexpectedly. --allowedTools 'file_read,grep,glob' restricts to read-only tools for a review step. file_write and file_delete are completely unnecessary for code review. Minimum footprint principle applies especially in non-interactive execution.
whyWrong: 20 tools degrades selection reliability AND includes dangerous write/delete tools for a read-only review
This is the correct answer
The review report can be written by the pipeline wrapper, not by Claude's file_write tool
Headless mode does NOT automatically restrict tools — you must specify --allowedTools explicitly

question: Which review criterion is specific and verifiable vs vague and aspirational?
options: 'Write clean, readable code'
'Functions exceeding 40 lines are flagged as Medium severity, with the exception of test setup functions'
'Handle errors appropriately in all async operations'
'Use best practices for this technology stack'
explanation: Specific and verifiable: 'functions exceeding 40 lines = Medium severity, except test setup functions.' A reviewer can count lines, check the function type, and apply the rule consistently. The other options ('clean', 'appropriate', 'best practices') are subjective — Claude's interpretation varies from human expectations, producing false positives.
whyWrong: 'Clean and readable' is subjective — produces inconsistent results based on Claude's interpretation
This is the correct answer
'Appropriately' is vague — what's appropriate in one context is different from another
'Best practices' varies by interpreter — there's no objective standard Claude can apply consistently

question: After a major dependency upgrade, 12 tests are failing. What is the correct approach for fixing them?
options: Fix all 12 simultaneously in one Claude Code session
Fix them sequentially: one test at a time, verify it passes, verify no regressions, then proceed to the next
Modify the tests to match the new dependency behavior rather than fixing the source code
Generate new tests that pass with the new dependency and discard the failing ones
explanation: Sequential test resolution: fix one failing test, run that test to verify it passes, run the full suite to verify no regressions, then move to the next. Fixing all 12 simultaneously causes interactions between fixes that create new failures and make root cause analysis impossible. Tests are the spec — never modify them to match wrong behavior.
whyWrong: Simultaneous fixes cause interactions — fixing test 1 may break test 5, and you won't know which fix caused it
This is the correct answer
Tests define correct behavior — modifying them to match wrong implementation inverts the purpose of tests
Discarding failing tests loses the behavioral specifications they encode — must fix the source, not delete the spec

question: A code review severity system should include what for each severity level?
options: Only a definition of what qualifies for that severity
Definition, concrete example of what IS that severity, example of what is NOT that severity, and required action
A numerical score range that maps to the severity
A list of specific files or modules where that severity applies
explanation: Complete severity calibration requires: (1) definition — what makes something this severity, (2) positive example — what IS at this level, (3) negative example — what does NOT qualify (prevents false positives), (4) required action — what must happen with a finding at this level. Without negative examples, Claude over-flags.
whyWrong: Definition alone without examples produces inconsistent application — Claude interprets 'security issue' broadly
This is the correct answer
Numerical scores add bureaucracy but don't improve calibration — concrete examples do
File-specific severity rules would require constant maintenance as code changes

question: CLAUDE.md includes: 'Use async/await for all database calls.' A session system prompt says: 'This is a sync-only microservice, use synchronous database calls.' Which instruction takes precedence?
options: CLAUDE.md always takes precedence — it's the project standard
The session system prompt takes precedence — runtime context overrides CLAUDE.md for specific sessions
They cannot conflict — CLAUDE.md should be updated to remove contradictions
Claude averages between both instructions
explanation: Runtime session prompts override CLAUDE.md for specific session instructions. CLAUDE.md provides general project conventions. The session system prompt provides task-specific context. When they conflict, the more specific runtime instruction wins. This is the intended hierarchy — CLAUDE.md sets defaults, sessions can override for specific contexts.
whyWrong: CLAUDE.md is a default — session prompts override it for specific tasks
This is the correct answer
Conflicts between layers are expected and resolved by hierarchy — runtime wins for specific task context
Claude follows the hierarchy — runtime > project CLAUDE.md. No averaging.

question: A review pipeline generates findings in plain text. The CI/CD script must decide whether to block or allow the merge. What is the fundamental problem?
options: Plain text is harder to read than JSON for humans
Plain text cannot be reliably parsed for automated gating decisions — structured JSON with severity fields is required
The CI/CD script needs to call the Claude API directly instead of using Claude Code
Plain text findings should be converted to PDF for archival
explanation: Automated merge gating requires machine-parseable output. Plain text with 'CRITICAL: SQL injection in line 47' cannot be reliably parsed — the format varies, phrasing changes, and regex is fragile. --output-format json with a defined schema ({findings: [{severity, file, line, issue}]}) enables reliable programmatic gating decisions.
whyWrong: Human readability is secondary to machine parseability for automated CI/CD decisions
This is the correct answer
Claude Code with --output-format json is the correct tool — direct API calls add unnecessary complexity
PDF conversion adds complexity and doesn't enable automated parsing

question: A developer asks Claude Code to add error handling to all async functions across a 150-file codebase. Should this use plan mode?
options: No — the task is straightforward; direct execution is always faster
Yes — 150 files, multiple valid approaches (wrapping vs middleware vs decorators), requires review before modifying 150 files
Only if the developer hasn't done it before
Only if the codebase has tests that could fail
explanation: Plan mode triggers: 45+ files affected (150 files), multiple valid architectural approaches (try/catch wrapping, error middleware, error decorators, error boundaries), and high risk if inconsistent patterns are mixed. The plan enables the developer to review and approve the approach before 150 files are modified.
whyWrong: Direct execution for 150 files risks inconsistent implementation and is hard to review before commit
This is the correct answer
Task familiarity doesn't determine plan mode — scope and approach complexity do
Test failures are a concern but not the reason to use plan mode — the scope and approach decisions are
